content.text: Testing agentic AI systems presents a unique challenge: the system's behaviour depends on LLM responses that are expensive, slow, and non-deterministic, while it also interacts with external tools — search engines, databases, file systems, APIs — that carry real side effects. Mocking and fixtures solve this by replacing those external dependencies with controlled substitutes during test execution.
is an object that stands in for a real dependency at runtime. When code calls
expecting an Anthropic API response, a mock intercepts that call and returns a value chosen by the test author. The real network is never touched. A

is a piece of pre-built test data — a saved JSON blob of what the API would have returned, a pre-populated database record, or a shared object set up once and injected into every test that needs it. Together, mocks and fixtures give a test suite full control over the agent's environment.
In the Python ecosystem, the primary tools are
decorator, and purpose-built HTTP-level libraries such as
patches Python objects in memory, while HTTP-level libraries intercept outgoing requests at the transport layer.

Agentic systems that run LLM API calls on every test are impractical to test at scale. A suite of 200 tests, each making two API calls at 1–2 seconds apiece with network latency included, takes upward of seven minutes and costs real money on every CI run. More critically, the non-determinism of LLM outputs makes assertions fragile: a test that checks whether the agent answered "Paris" may pass 19 out of 20 runs and fail on the twentieth for no reproducible reason.

Mocking solves both problems at once. A mocked LLM client returns a predetermined answer in microseconds. There is no network hop, no API key required, no quota consumed. The test result is 100% reproducible: given the same inputs and the same mocked responses, the agent will always produce the same output, making failures meaningful and debuggable rather than random noise.

Beyond cost and speed, mocking enables scenarios that are difficult or impossible to trigger reliably against a real API: simulating a rate-limit error, an empty response, a malformed tool-call block, a timeout, or a refusal. These edge cases must be tested, and mocks make them trivially reproducible.
A mock returns data in microseconds. A full agentic test suite of hundreds of tests can complete in under 10 seconds versus minutes against a live API.
Pre-specified responses eliminate LLM non-determinism. Every test run on the same code produces the same result, making CI green/red signals reliable.
No API tokens are consumed. This matters for tight budgets and for running tests on every pull-request push without accumulating charges.
Tests pass without internet access, enabling local development on planes or in air-gapped environments.
Error conditions — rate limits, malformed JSON, empty content blocks, mid-stream disconnects — can be injected deterministically, something a live API does not reliably reproduce on demand.
Each test exercises exactly one code path. Side effects to real databases or external services are eliminated, so tests do not interfere with each other or corrupt production data.
Because tests share no real external state, they can run in parallel (e.g. with
The central risk of mocking is

: the mock no longer accurately represents the real system it replaces. If the Anthropic SDK changes the shape of a response object, or a tool's API changes its return format, mocks built against the old shape continue to pass while the real code breaks. Mocks require active maintenance whenever upstream interfaces evolve.

breaks the moment the caller changes the model name, even if the logic being tested has nothing to do with model selection. Tests should assert what matters, not every detail of how a dependency was called.
JSON fixture files saved from the API today may not reflect the API six months from now. Include fixture refresh in the same workflow that updates the SDK version.

A test suite that mocks every external call passes even when the integration between the agent and the real API is broken. Complement unit tests with a small number of real integration tests run on a schedule.
Reproducing a complex SDK response shape — with nested content blocks, stop reasons, usage metadata — takes careful setup. Mistakes in mock construction can make tests pass for the wrong reason.
Mocks do not simulate network delays. Performance problems at real API latency are invisible in a mocked test suite.
module works by replacing an attribute on a Python object with a
for the duration of a test. When used as a context manager via
, the original attribute is restored on exit:
namespace and replaces it with a
. Any code in that module that calls
receives the mock class, and any call on its return value (the mock instance) is recorded and can return pre-programmed values.
Pytest fixtures use a different mechanism: the

decorator registers a function as a fixture factory. When a test function declares a parameter with the same name as a registered fixture, pytest calls the factory and passes the result as the argument. Fixtures can be scoped (
) to control how often they are recreated. A
-scoped fixture is set up once per test run; a
-scoped fixture is torn down after every individual test.
The two mechanisms compose naturally. A fixture can use

as its implementation, yielding the mock to the test and cleaning up afterward. This pattern — a fixture that patches, yields, and unpatch — is the idiomatic way to share mock setup across a test class without repeating boilerplate.

attrs.alt: Mocking and fixtures architecture: Test Suite directs three test doubles (Mock LLM Client, Fixture Store, Tool Stubs) which feed the Agent Under Test, whose output flows to Assertions. The Real LLM API is crossed out, indicating it is bypassed.

content.text: The diagram shows the three categories of test double used in agentic AI testing. The
intercepts calls to the Anthropic (or OpenAI) SDK and returns canned text or tool-use responses from the Fixture Store. The
holds pre-authored JSON blobs that mirror real API responses; these are loaded by conftest.py fixtures and injected into mocks.
replace external integrations — web search, database queries, file operations — with functions that return deterministic outputs without touching the network. The
runs its real production logic against all three doubles, and the test then asserts on its output.

that verify the agent's decision-making logic: does it call the right tool given a particular LLM response? Does it handle a rate-limit error by retrying? Does it correctly parse a tool-call block and format the result before appending it to the message history? These scenarios require precise control over what the LLM returns, which only mocking can provide.
the test verifies routing, error handling, state transitions, or prompt construction — any logic internal to the agent that does not depend on genuine model quality.
multiple tests share the same API response structure, or when reproducing a specific failure observed in production requires an exact reproduction of the response that caused it.
the goal is to evaluate the quality of the model's answers. Prompt engineering evaluations and model-quality benchmarks must run against real model APIs.

testing the integration boundary between the agent and a third-party API for the first time. Write one live integration test to validate the SDK shape, then save its output as a fixture for all subsequent unit tests.
— tests that make real API calls — to a small, slow suite run on a schedule or before release, not on every pull-request push.
Identify every external dependency the agent uses: the LLM client, tool functions, any storage layer.
Inject those dependencies into the agent via constructor arguments or function parameters rather than importing them at module level. Dependency injection is the prerequisite for mocking.
directory. Save representative real API responses there as JSON files. Name them by scenario:
fixture that patches the SDK and yields the mock instance.
For tool stubs, write simple Python classes or functions with the same signature as the real tool. Return hardcoded or fixture-loaded data.
In each test, request the fixtures as parameters. Configure the mock's
to script the LLM's responses for the scenario under test.
Assert on the agent's output and, where relevant, on which dependencies were called and with what arguments.
The key architectural rule is to keep
at the core of the agent design. An agent that creates its own

client internally cannot be tested without patching that specific import path. An agent that accepts a client object at construction time can be tested by passing a mock directly, with no patching required.

# Basic mock: intercept an Anthropic API call in a unit test
# pip install anthropic pytest

from unittest.mock import MagicMock, patch
import anthropic

# --- module under test ---
def ask_agent(question: str, client: anthropic.Anthropic) -> str:
    """Thin wrapper around the Anthropic messages API."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# --- test ---
def test_ask_agent_returns_llm_text():
    mock_client = MagicMock(spec=anthropic.Anthropic)

    # Build a fake response that mirrors the real SDK shape
    fake_block = MagicMock()
    fake_block.text = "The capital of France is Paris."
    fake_response = MagicMock()
    fake_response.content = [fake_block]
    mock_client.messages.create.return_value = fake_response

    result = ask_agent("What is the capital of France?", mock_client)

    assert result == "The capital of France is Paris."
    mock_client.messages.create.assert_called_once_with(
        model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is the capital of France?"}],
    )
Intermediate: pytest Fixtures and Tool Stubs

# conftest.py — shared pytest fixtures for an agentic AI test suite
import json
import pytest
from pathlib import Path
from unittest.mock import MagicMock, patch
import anthropic

FIXTURES_DIR = Path(__file__).parent / "fixtures"

@pytest.fixture
def mock_anthropic_client():
    """Returns a MagicMock that replaces anthropic.Anthropic in every test."""
    with patch("anthropic.Anthropic") as MockClass:
        instance = MockClass.return_value
        yield instance

@pytest.fixture
def canned_search_results():
    """Load a pre-built list of search results from a JSON fixture file."""
    data = json.loads((FIXTURES_DIR / "search_results.json").read_text())
    return data["results"]

@pytest.fixture
def stub_search_tool(canned_search_results):
    """Callable that mimics a web-search tool without making real HTTP requests."""
    def _search(query: str, max_results: int = 5):
        # Return a deterministic slice regardless of the query
        return canned_search_results[:max_results]
    return _search

@pytest.fixture
def tool_use_response(mock_anthropic_client):
    """
    Pre-configure mock_anthropic_client to emit a tool-use block on the first
    call, then a final text answer on the second call (simulating one tool turn).
    """
    first = MagicMock()
    first.stop_reason = "tool_use"
    tool_block = MagicMock()
    tool_block.type = "tool_use"
    tool_block.name = "web_search"
    tool_block.id = "tu_001"
    tool_block.input = {"query": "latest Python release"}
    first.content = [tool_block]

    second = MagicMock()
    second.stop_reason = "end_turn"
    text_block = MagicMock()
    text_block.type = "text"
    text_block.text = "Python 3.13 was released in October 2024."
    second.content = [text_block]

    mock_anthropic_client.messages.create.side_effect = [first, second]
    return mock_anthropic_client

Consider a production research agent that accepts a user question, optionally invokes a web-search tool, and returns a synthesized answer. In CI, every push runs the full unit test suite in under 15 seconds. Here is how the test suite for that agent is structured:

# tests/test_research_agent.py — full integration-style test using stubs
import pytest
from agent.research_agent import ResearchAgent  # the module under test

class FakeSearchTool:
    """Deterministic search stub: returns fixed results for any query."""
    RESULTS = [
        {"title": "Python 3.13 Release Notes", "url": "https://docs.python.org/3.13/", "snippet": "Released October 2024."},
        {"title": "What is new in Python 3.13", "url": "https://docs.python.org/3.13/whatsnew/3.13.html", "snippet": "Free-threaded CPython, JIT compiler preview."},
    ]

    def __call__(self, query: str, max_results: int = 5):
        return self.RESULTS[:max_results]


def test_research_agent_uses_search_result_in_answer(tool_use_response):
    """
    Scenario:
    - Agent receives a question about the latest Python release.
    - Mock LLM emits a tool-use block (web_search) on turn 1.
    - Stub search tool returns pre-built results.
    - Mock LLM uses those results to produce the final text answer on turn 2.
    - We assert the final answer contains the expected version string.
    """
    agent = ResearchAgent(
        client=tool_use_response,
        tools={"web_search": FakeSearchTool()},
    )

    answer = agent.run("What is the latest Python release?")

    assert "3.13" in answer
    # Verify the LLM was called exactly twice (one tool turn + one final turn)
    assert tool_use_response.messages.create.call_count == 2


@pytest.mark.parametrize("question,expected_fragment", [
    ("Latest Python?", "3.13"),
    ("Python version?", "3.13"),
])
def test_research_agent_parametrized(tool_use_response, question, expected_fragment):
    agent = ResearchAgent(client=tool_use_response, tools={"web_search": FakeSearchTool()})
    answer = agent.run(question)
    assert expected_fragment in answer
fixture (defined in conftest.py) scripts a two-turn LLM conversation: the first call returns a tool-use block requesting

; the test framework feeds the stub search results back to the agent; the second call returns the final text answer. The agent's real orchestration logic — the loop that handles tool calls — executes unchanged. The only substituted parts are the network calls.

This test suite runs in a CI pipeline on every pull request, with no API keys in the environment, completing in about 8 seconds for 50 tests. A separate scheduled workflow runs 5 end-to-end tests against the live API once per day, catching any drift between the mocks and the real API.
Think of mocking and fixtures as a

. When training pilots, you do not put them in a real aircraft over a real city to test their emergency procedures. You build a cockpit that looks, feels, and responds exactly like a real one, but where the instructor controls what the instruments show. Smoke in the cabin, engine failure, sudden crosswind: all are injected at will, repeatedly, safely.

The agent is the pilot. The mock LLM client and tool stubs are the simulator cockpit. The fixture files are the instructor's scenario scripts. The agent executes its real decision-making logic — the code you actually care about testing — while the environment is 100% under your control. Just as a simulator pass does not replace a solo flight, a mocked test suite does not replace a live integration test; but it is essential groundwork, enabling systematic coverage of every failure mode before the aircraft ever leaves the ground.
in the wrong namespace is the most common setup error. Python's import system makes the object that is patched the one that was already imported, not the original class. If the module under test does
, patch the path as seen by that module:
break whenever an unrelated parameter changes. Assert only on the parts that matter to the test's purpose. Use
when the test cares that the LLM was called exactly once but not about the exact parameters.

A fixture built from an SDK version six months ago may have a different response shape than the current SDK. If the agent's response-parsing code was updated to match the new shape, but the fixture still has the old shape, the test will pass against the (now wrong) fixture while the real code breaks against the live API. Include fixture regeneration as a step in SDK upgrade checklists.

It is straightforward to mock the happy path. Teams frequently skip injecting error responses — timeouts, HTTP 429 rate limits, malformed JSON in a tool-call block — because those are harder to set up. These are precisely the cases most likely to cause silent failures in production. Use
exercises the retry logic without ever hitting the API.
Pass the LLM client and all tool callables as constructor arguments. Do not instantiate them inside the agent. This single architectural decision unlocks all mocking strategies.
Keep fixture files small and named by scenario.
A fixture file should represent one specific LLM response scenario:
. Small fixtures are easier to update when the API changes.
for expensive, read-only test data (fixture files loaded from disk). Use
(the default) for mocks that must be reset between tests to avoid state leaking across test cases.
Most agentic flows involve two or more LLM calls. Use
to return a different value on each successive call, mirroring the real agent loop.
Run one live API call per SDK upgrade to verify the mock shape is still accurate. Save the result as an updated fixture. Automate this in a nightly CI job.
restricts the mock to the attributes and methods that exist on the real class, catching typos and invalid attribute accesses that a bare MagicMock would silently accept.
Separate test data from test logic.
Keep fixture JSON files in a dedicated
directory. Load them in conftest.py fixtures, never inline large blobs in test functions.

A test suite built on mocks has near-zero I/O overhead. The dominant cost is Python interpreter time: object creation, attribute lookups, and assertion evaluation. In practice, a suite of 200 mocked agent tests runs in 5–15 seconds on modern hardware.

amortize expensive setup across the entire run. Loading fixture JSON files from disk is fast, but if fixture construction involves computation (building a large message history, generating token counts), session scope can reduce that to a one-time cost.
Because mocked tests share no real external state, they are trivially safe to parallelize with
). On a four-core machine, four-way parallelism can cut wall-clock time by 60–70%.
instance records every call made on it. A test that drives an agent through hundreds of LLM turns without clearing the mock accumulates a large call record. Reset mocks between tests with
or by using function-scoped fixtures that create a fresh mock per test.

A session-scoped fixture that loads all fixture files once at suite startup eliminates repeated disk reads. For very large fixture libraries, consider loading lazily (only when first requested by a test).
The standard library's mocking framework. Provides
(for async LLM clients). No additional installation required.
The de-facto Python test runner. The
) together form the fixture infrastructure used by most agentic AI projects.

An HTTPX-level mock library that intercepts HTTP requests made by HTTPX (which the Anthropic Python SDK uses under the hood). Useful for testing streaming responses and for projects that prefer not to patch at the Python-object level. Supports async out of the box.
library. Less relevant for the Anthropic SDK (which uses HTTPX by default) but still used in projects that depend on
Runs pytest tests in parallel across multiple CPU cores. Especially valuable for large mocked test suites where each test is fast but the volume is high.
Required when the agent code is async. Provides the

Record real HTTP interactions on the first run, then replay them on subsequent runs. A middle ground between live integration tests and hand-crafted mocks. Useful for generating initial fixtures automatically.

Test data factories that generate realistic but fake objects. Useful for creating realistic message histories, tool-call structures, or user profile data without depending on specific real-world records.

Unit tests with mocks excel for logic correctness. Integration tests catch drift between mocks and the real API. VCR recording is a pragmatic middle ground: record once, replay always. Evaluation harnesses measure model quality rather than code correctness. Snapshot testing locks in output format but does not validate logic paths.

Fixture files that mirror real API responses may inadvertently contain sensitive data. If the fixture was captured from a session where the agent processed personal information, user credentials, or internal business data, that data now lives in a plaintext JSON file committed to the repository. Review every fixture file before committing. Scrub or replace any personal identifiers, API keys, or proprietary content with placeholder values.

Mock tests require no API keys at all. Ensure CI job configurations do not pass real keys to the test environment when running the mocked suite. This prevents accidental token consumption and removes the attack surface entirely.
accepts any attribute access silently, which means tests can pass even when the code accesses a nonexistent SDK attribute. The spec enforces structural correctness.
as a context manager or fixture, not as a module-level decorator that applies permanently. A patch left active beyond its intended scope can affect other tests and produce confusing failures.
Test retry and backoff logic explicitly.
Agentic systems typically implement retry logic for rate limits and transient errors. Script
to raise the relevant exceptions on the first N calls and then return a valid response, confirming that the retry logic actually works before relying on it in production.

Establish a policy: when a new SDK minor version is released, regenerate the fixture files that cover the API shape. Include this step in the SDK upgrade checklist. Stale fixtures are a silent reliability hazard.
Mocks replace real dependencies (LLM clients, tool functions) at the Python-object level, making tests fast, deterministic, and free; fixtures supply the pre-built data those mocks return.
Dependency injection is the prerequisite: an agent that accepts its client and tools as constructor arguments can be tested by passing mocks directly, with no import-path patching required.
on the mock to reproduce the agent's full plan-act-observe loop in tests.
to catch invalid attribute accesses that would silently pass without the spec constraint.
Keep fixture files in a dedicated directory, name them by scenario, and include fixture refresh as a step whenever the SDK or tool API is upgraded to prevent silent drift.
Complement the mocked unit suite with a small scheduled integration test that makes real API calls — this catches any divergence between the mock shape and the live API before it reaches production.
Review fixture files for sensitive data before committing; mocked test environments should contain no real API keys, personal data, or internal business information.
